clean_story:
Tiny steps. Giant mental maps.

"Ant" comes from emete, an Old English word for the insect’s relentless work—but their real skill is navigation without landmarks.

A desert ant leaves its nest, twists 200 meters through featureless sand, then returns in a straight line. No GPS. No breadcrumbs. It counts steps like a pedometer: 10,000 strides out means 10,000 back. Lose a leg? It misjudges by exactly that leg’s stride length. Sunlight is its compass. Even under cloud cover, ants detect polarized light—invisible patterns in the sky—like reading a hidden grid. If you move an ant’s food mid-journey, it marches to where the food was, proving it follows memory, not scent.

City ants use smell differently. Pavement ants drag their abdomens to leave pheromone trails, but only after scouting randomly first. Their paths look messy until one short route emerges, optimized by thousands of tiny errors.

Three related subjects are insect vision secrets, swarm math rules, and how robots copy ants.
